skill.name: image-to-text-pdf
skill.description: Convert a finished raster image into an image-based PDF with an invisible selectable, copyable, and searchable text layer. Ideal for posters, visual resumes, and generated graphics where the image is the visual source of truth.
skill.summary: Add invisible selectable text layers to raster images and export them as searchable PDFs.
skill.author: zjsxply

security_audit.summary: All 49 static findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The external_commands detections are backtick characters inside markdown documentation and code examples, not actual shell execution. The weak cryptographic algorithm detections are false pattern matches on argument parsing and text processing code with no cryptographic operations present. The obfuscation detection is a legitimate Unicode range check for CJK characters. The system reconnaissance detection is a RuntimeError string about text fitting. The skill performs only local file I/O on user-specified paths and contains no network access, no external command execution, and no data exfiltration.

content.user_title: Create Searchable PDFs from Images with Invisible Text Layers
content.value_statement: Raster images like posters and visual resumes look great but cannot be searched or copied. This skill preserves the original image exactly while embedding an invisible text layer, producing a PDF that is both visually faithful and fully searchable.
actual_capabilities: Generate an image-based PDF with an invisible selectable text layer overlaid on the original image
Produce a debug inspection PDF with visible text boxes and highlights for verification
Convert OCR word-level bounding boxes into line-level layout JSON
Support CJK and non-Latin text through custom font embedding
Accept multiple OCR JSON formats including Google Vision and generic word lists
Correct OCR text against an authoritative source text file using fuzzy matching
limitations: Requires PyMuPDF and Pillow to be installed in the local Python environment
Does not perform OCR itself; requires external OCR or vision model output as input
Text layer geometry is approximate and may need manual adjustment for complex layouts
Rotated or curved text may require multiple smaller bounding boxes

use_cases.title: Make Generated Posters Searchable
use_cases.description: Designers and marketers generate poster images with AI tools. This skill lets them export the final image as a PDF that recruiters and clients can search and copy text from.

use_cases.title: Export Visual Resumes with Copyable Text
use_cases.description: Job seekers create visually rich resumes as images. This skill adds a hidden text layer so applicant tracking systems and recruiters can parse the content.

use_cases.title: Archive Scanned Documents as Searchable PDFs
use_cases.description: Researchers and archivists scan printed materials. After running OCR, this skill composes the scanned image and recognized text into a clean searchable PDF.

prompt_templates.title: Convert an image to a searchable PDF
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an image at /path/to/poster.png. Please convert it into a searchable PDF with an invisible text layer. Use OCR to extract text and box positions, then run the compose script to generate the final PDF and a debug PDF so I can verify the text placement.

prompt_templates.title: Create a searchable PDF from a visual resume
prompt_templates.prompt: I generated a visual resume as resume.png and I have the original source text in source.txt. Extract the text positions with OCR, correct the text using source.txt, and produce both a final invisible-text PDF and a debug inspection PDF.

prompt_templates.title: Build a CJK searchable PDF with custom fonts
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a Chinese poster at poster.png. Run OCR to get word boxes, convert them to line-level layout JSON, and compose a searchable PDF using /path/to/NotoSansCJK-Regular.ttc as the font. Generate both the final PDF and the debug version.

prompt_templates.title: Batch process multiple images with source text correction
prompt_templates.prompt: I have three images (page1.png, page2.png, page3.png) and a single source text file (content.txt). For each image, extract OCR word boxes, group them into lines, correct the text against the closest matching line from content.txt, and compose a searchable PDF. Output final PDFs and debug PDFs for all three pages.

output_examples.input: A generated poster image with title, subtitle, and body text blocks.
output_examples.output: A PDF that looks identical to the poster but allows the user to select and copy every text block. A second debug PDF shows red-outlined boxes with pink visible text where the invisible layer will be placed.

output_examples.input: OCR JSON with word-level bounding boxes and a source text file.
output_examples.output: A layout JSON where each item contains a line of corrected text and a bounding box. The text matches the authoritative source, while the box positions come from the original OCR.

best_practices: Always generate and inspect the debug PDF before delivering the final invisible-text PDF. Misaligned boxes are easier to fix in the layout JSON than after delivery.
Use line-level text boxes instead of paragraph-level boxes unless the paragraph must be selected as a single unit. Line-level boxes are easier to position accurately.
Keep the generated image as the visual layer rather than rebuilding the layout in HTML or LaTeX. This preserves exact visual fidelity while the text layer handles searchability.
anti_patterns: Do not try to match the rasterized font exactly in the invisible layer. The hidden text only needs close geometry and correct content, not pixel-perfect font matching.
Do not embed OCR mistakes into the final PDF. Use the source text or user-provided text to correct transcription errors before composing the PDF.
Do not skip the debug PDF for dense or complex layouts. Tiny text and tightly packed regions often need manual adjustment that is only visible in the inspection output.

faq.question: Does this skill perform OCR on its own?
faq.answer: No. The skill expects OCR output from an external tool or vision model. It provides scripts to convert OCR word boxes into layout JSON and to compose the final PDF.

faq.question: What Python dependencies are required?
faq.answer: PyMuPDF and Pillow are required. The scripts will print an installation command if either library is missing.

faq.question: Why is the text in the final PDF invisible?
faq.answer: The text is rendered with a special mode that makes it selectable and copyable without appearing visually. The original image remains the only visible content, preserving exact visual fidelity.

faq.question: Can I use this with non-Latin languages like Chinese or Japanese?
faq.answer: Yes. Pass a Unicode font file using the --font-file argument. The script will warn you if non-Latin text is detected and no font file is provided.

faq.question: What is the debug PDF for?
faq.answer: The debug PDF shows highlighted text boxes and visible text where the invisible layer will be placed. It is used to verify that text boxes are aligned correctly before generating the final PDF.

faq.question: What OCR JSON formats are supported?
faq.answer: The converter accepts common formats with words, items, textAnnotations, or nested page, block, paragraph, and line objects. It also supports boundingBox, boundingPoly, polygon, and simple x-y-width-height coordinates.
